question_title: Will Germany’s seasonally and calendar adjusted real new orders in manufacturing increase month over month in February 2026?
summary: Germany's February 2026 manufacturing new orders data (not yet released; expected early April from Destatis) follows a -11.1% MoM drop in January that was almost entirely large-order driven (ex-large-orders was only -0.4%). The strongest signal for a rebound is the February Manufacturing PMI at 50.7 — the first expansion in 3.5 years — with "robust" new order growth and export orders returning to growth. However, the historical base rate for positive MoM changes is only 40% over the past 2 years, and in both 2024 and 2025, February failed to bounce after big January drops (though PMI was in contraction those years, unlike now). The Middle East conflict began Feb 28, leaving most of February unaffected. I estimate 60% probability, reflecting the genuine tension between the strong PMI leading indicator and the volatile, large-order-driven nature of the Destatis series.

factors.description: February 2026 Manufacturing PMI at 50.7 (expansion) with robust new order growth — first expansion in 3.5 years. Strongest leading indicator for Destatis orders.
January 2026 drop was large-order driven: headline -11.1% but ex-large-orders only -0.4%. Large orders are volatile and mean-reverting.
Historical base rate: only 40% of months show positive MoM changes over 2024-2026 (10/25 months). Mean MoM is -0.22%.
After similar big January drops in 2024 (-11.3%) and 2025 (-5.0%), February showed no bounce (0.0% and -0.4%).
factors.conditional: PMI was in contraction in those years, unlike 2026's expansion

factors.description: Middle East conflict started Feb 28 — most of February unaffected. March PMI shows front-loading which may have started in late Feb.
Structural economic weakness: ifo business climate fell to 86.4 in March; bankruptcies at 11-year high; exports fell 2.3% in January.
March 2026 PMI rose further to 51.7 with 4-year high in new orders, confirming February recovery trend was genuine.
TradingEconomics end-of-quarter forecast is +1.10%, suggesting analysts expect positive rebound.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 60% probability YES** (logit: 0.41)

Key reasoning:
- **Strongest YES signal**: February PMI at 50.7 with robust new orders — first manufacturing expansion in 3.5 years. This is qualitatively different from prior years when Feb followed big Jan drops but PMI was in contraction.
- **Mean reversion**: January's -11.1% was almost entirely large-order driven (ex-large was only -0.4%), creating a depressed base that favors a bounce.
- **Key uncertainty**: The Destatis series is notoriously volatile due to large-order swings. A single contract can move the headline by several percentage points regardless of broad conditions. This prevents going much above 60% despite the positive PMI signal.
- **Base rate anchor**: Only 40% of months showed positive MoM changes over the past 2 years, and February specifically failed to bounce after big January drops in both 2024 and 2025.
sources_consulted: [New orders in manufacturing in January 2026: -11.1% on the previous month](https://www.destatis.de/EN/Press/2026/03/PE26_072_421.html)
[Germany February flash manufacturing PMI 50.7 vs 49.5 expected | investingLive](https://investinglive.com/news/germany-february-flash-manufacturing-pmi-507-vs-495-expected-20260220/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the first official Destatis value for February 2026 shows a positive month-over-month change in seasonally and calendar adjusted real new orders in manufacturing. It resolves to no if that first official value is 0.0% or lower. The primary source is Destatis’s “Volume index of New orders, manufacturing total” page at https://www.destatis.de/EN/Themes/Economy/Short-Term-Indicators/New-Orders/kae211.html
fine_print: Use the first published February 2026 value and ignore later revisions unless Destatis corrects the initial release before May 1, 2026. The relevant series is the total manufacturing index, not domestic-only or foreign-only orders. Candidate Sources: Destatis industry press releases hub https://www.destatis.de/EN/Themes/Economic-Sectors-Enterprises/Industry-Manufacturing/_node.html ; Destatis press releases page https://www.destatis.de/EN/Press/press_node.html
